title: 2008 financial crisis

infobox.key: Part of a series on the

infobox.value: | * [v](/wiki/Template:Great_Recession_series "Template:Great Recession series") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Great_Recession_series "Template talk:Great Recession series") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Great_Recession_series "Special:EditPage/Template:Great Recession series")

(**GFC**) or the **Panic of 2008**, was a major worldwide [financial crisis](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") centered in the United States. The causes included excessive [speculation](/wiki/Speculation "Speculation") on property values by both homeowners and [financial institutions](/wiki/Financial_institution "Financial institution"), leading to the [2000s United States housing bubble](/wiki/2000s_United_States_housing_bubble "2000s United States housing bubble"). This was exacerbated by [predatory lending](/wiki/Predatory_lending "Predatory lending") for [subprime mortgages](/wiki/Subprime_mortgages "Subprime mortgages") and by deficiencies in [regulation](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation"). [Cash out refinancings](/wiki/Cash_out_refinancing "Cash out refinancing") had fueled an increase in consumption that could no longer be sustained when home prices declined. The first phase of the crisis was the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), which began in early 2007, as [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities") (MBS) tied to U.S. [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), and a vast web of [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)") linked to those MBS, collapsed in value. A [liquidity crisis](/wiki/Liquidity_crisis "Liquidity crisis") spread to global institutions by mid-2007 and climaxed with the [bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_Lehman_Brothers "Bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers") in September 2008, which triggered a [stock market crash](/wiki/Stock_market_crash "Stock market crash") and [bank runs](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") in several countries.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The crisis exacerbated the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), a [global recession](/wiki/Global_recession "Global recession") that began in mid-2007, as well as the [United States bear market of 2007–2009](/wiki/United_States_bear_market_of_2007%E2%80%932009 "United States bear market of 2007–2009"). It was also a contributor to the [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis") and the [euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Worldwide economic crisis

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Lehman_Brothers_Times_Square_by_David_Shankbone.jpg/250px-Lehman_Brothers_Times_Square_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lehman_Brothers_Times_Square_by_David_Shankbone.jpg)

The [bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_Lehman_Brothers "Bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers") ([headquarters](/wiki/745_Seventh_Avenue "745 Seventh Avenue") pictured), the fourth-largest U.S. investment bank (behind [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley"), and [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch")), on September 15, 2008, is often considered the climax of the 2008 financial crisis.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/TED_Spread.png/330px-TED_Spread.png)](/wiki/File:TED_Spread.png)

The [TED spread](/wiki/TED_spread "TED spread"), an indicator of perceived [credit risk](/wiki/Credit_risk "Credit risk") in the financial system, increased significantly during the crisis. It spiked sharply in August 2007, remained volatile for a year, and spiked even higher in September 2008 to reach a record 4.65% on October 10, 2008.

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Great_Recession_series "Template:Great Recession series") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Great_Recession_series "Template talk:Great Recession series") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Great_Recession_series "Special:EditPage/Template:Great Recession series") |

The **2008 financial crisis**, also known as the **global financial crisis** (**GFC**) or the **Panic of 2008**, was a major worldwide [financial crisis](/wiki/Financial_crisis "Financial crisis") centered in the United States. The causes included excessive [speculation](/wiki/Speculation "Speculation") on property values by both homeowners and [financial institutions](/wiki/Financial_institution "Financial institution"), leading to the [2000s United States housing bubble](/wiki/2000s_United_States_housing_bubble "2000s United States housing bubble"). This was exacerbated by [predatory lending](/wiki/Predatory_lending "Predatory lending") for [subprime mortgages](/wiki/Subprime_mortgages "Subprime mortgages") and by deficiencies in [regulation](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation"). [Cash out refinancings](/wiki/Cash_out_refinancing "Cash out refinancing") had fueled an increase in consumption that could no longer be sustained when home prices declined. The first phase of the crisis was the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), which began in early 2007, as [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities") (MBS) tied to U.S. [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), and a vast web of [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)") linked to those MBS, collapsed in value. A [liquidity crisis](/wiki/Liquidity_crisis "Liquidity crisis") spread to global institutions by mid-2007 and climaxed with the [bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_Lehman_Brothers "Bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers") in September 2008, which triggered a [stock market crash](/wiki/Stock_market_crash "Stock market crash") and [bank runs](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") in several countries.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The crisis exacerbated the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), a [global recession](/wiki/Global_recession "Global recession") that began in mid-2007, as well as the [United States bear market of 2007–2009](/wiki/United_States_bear_market_of_2007%E2%80%932009 "United States bear market of 2007–2009"). It was also a contributor to the [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis") and the [euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis").

During the 1990s, the [U.S. Congress](/wiki/U.S._Congress "U.S. Congress") had passed legislation that intended to expand [affordable housing](/wiki/Affordable_housing_in_the_United_States "Affordable housing in the United States") through looser financing rules, and in 1999, parts of the [1933 Banking Act](/wiki/1933_Banking_Act "1933 Banking Act") (Glass–Steagall Act) were [repealed](/wiki/Gramm%E2%80%93Leach%E2%80%93Bliley_Act "Gramm–Leach–Bliley Act"), enabling institutions to mix low-risk operations, such as [commercial banking](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank") and [insurance](/wiki/Insurance#Insurance_companies "Insurance"), with higher-risk operations such as [investment banking](/wiki/Investment_banking "Investment banking") and [proprietary trading](/wiki/Proprietary_trading "Proprietary trading").[[2]](#cite_note-2) As the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") ("Fed") lowered the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") from 2000 to 2003, institutions increasingly targeted low-income homebuyers, largely belonging to [racial minorities](/wiki/Racial_minorities "Racial minorities"), with high-risk loans;[[3]](#cite_note-3) this development went unattended by regulators.[[4]](#cite_note-4) As interest rates rose from 2004 to 2006, the cost of [mortgages](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage") rose and the demand for housing fell; in early 2007, as more U.S. subprime mortgage holders began [defaulting](/wiki/Default_(finance) "Default (finance)") on their repayments, lenders went bankrupt, culminating in the bankruptcy of [New Century Financial](/wiki/New_Century_Financial "New Century Financial") in April. As demand and prices continued to fall, the [financial contagion](/wiki/Financial_contagion "Financial contagion") spread to global [credit markets](/wiki/Credit_markets "Credit markets") by August 2007, and [central banks](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") began injecting [liquidity](/wiki/Liquidity "Liquidity"). In March 2008, [Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns"), the fifth-largest U.S. investment bank, was sold to [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") in a "[fire sale](/wiki/Fire_sale "Fire sale")" backed by Fed financing.

In response to the growing crisis, governments around the world deployed massive [bailouts](/wiki/Bailout "Bailout") of financial institutions and used [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") and [fiscal policies](/wiki/Fiscal_policies "Fiscal policies") to prevent an [economic collapse](/wiki/Economic_collapse "Economic collapse") of the [global financial system](/wiki/Global_financial_system "Global financial system").[[5]](#cite_note-5) By July 2008, [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae") and [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac"), companies which together owned or guaranteed half of the U.S. housing market, verged on collapse; the [Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008](/wiki/Housing_and_Economic_Recovery_Act_of_2008 "Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008") enabled the federal government to seize them on September 7. [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") (the fourth-largest U.S. investment bank) filed for the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history on September 15, which was followed by a Fed bail-out of [American International Group](/wiki/American_International_Group "American International Group") (the country's largest insurer) the next day, and the seizure of [Washington Mutual](/wiki/Washington_Mutual "Washington Mutual") in the largest bank failure in U.S. history on September 25. On October 3, Congress passed the [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act"), authorizing the [Treasury Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") to purchase [toxic assets](/wiki/Toxic_assets "Toxic assets") and bank stocks through the $700 billion [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program") (TARP). The Fed began a program of [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing") by buying treasury bonds and other assets, such as MBS, and the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act"), signed in February 2009 by newly elected president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), included a range of measures intended to preserve existing jobs and create new ones. These initiatives combined, coupled with actions taken in other countries, ended the worst of the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession") by mid-2009.

Assessments of the crisis's impact in the U.S. vary, but suggest that some 8.7 million jobs were lost, causing unemployment to rise from 5% in 2007 to a high of 10% in October 2009. The percentage of citizens living in poverty rose from 12.5% in 2007 to 15.1% in 2010. The [Dow Jones Industrial Average](/wiki/Dow_Jones_Industrial_Average "Dow Jones Industrial Average") fell by 53% between October 2007 and March 2009, and some estimates suggest that one in four households lost 75% or more of their [net worth](/wiki/Net_worth "Net worth"). In 2010, the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") was passed, overhauling financial regulations.[[6]](#cite_note-6) It was opposed by many [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), and it was weakened by the [Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Economic_Growth,_Regulatory_Relief,_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Economic Growth, Regulatory Relief, and Consumer Protection Act") in 2018. The [Basel III](/wiki/Basel_III "Basel III") capital and liquidity standards were also adopted by countries around the world.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/GDP_Real_Growth_in_2009.svg/500px-GDP_Real_Growth_in_2009.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_Real_Growth_in_2009.svg)

World map showing [real GDP growth rates](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_real_GDP_growth_rate "List of countries by real GDP growth rate") for 2009 (countries in brown were in recession)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/NYUGDPFinancialShare.jpg/500px-NYUGDPFinancialShare.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYUGDPFinancialShare.jpg)

Share in GDP of U.S. financial sector since 1860[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The crisis exacerbated the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), a [global recession](/wiki/Global_recession "Global recession") that began in mid-2007.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) It was also followed by the [euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis"), which began with the start of the [Greek government-debt crisis](/wiki/Greek_government-debt_crisis "Greek government-debt crisis") in late 2009, and the [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis"), which involved the [bank failure](/wiki/Bank_failure "Bank failure") of all three of the major banks in Iceland and, relative to the size of its economy, was the largest economic collapse suffered by any country in history.[[15]](#cite_note-15) It was among the five worst financial crises the world had experienced and led to a loss of more than $2 trillion from the global economy.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) U.S. home mortgage debt relative to [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") increased from an average of 46% during the 1990s to 73% during 2008, reaching $10.5 (~$15 trillion in 2024) trillion.[[18]](#cite_note-headache-18) The increase in [cash out refinancings](/wiki/Cash_out_refinancing "Cash out refinancing"), as home values rose, fueled an increase in consumption that could no longer be sustained when home prices declined.[[19]](#cite_note-extracted-19)[[20]](#cite_note-freecash-20)[[21]](#cite_note-boosted-21) Many financial institutions owned investments whose value was based on home mortgages such as [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities"), or [credit derivatives](/wiki/Credit_derivative "Credit derivative") used to insure them against failure, which declined in value significantly.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) The [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") estimated that large U.S. and European banks lost more than $1 trillion on [toxic assets](/wiki/Toxic_asset "Toxic asset") and from bad loans from January 2007 to September 2009.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Lack of investor confidence in bank [solvency](/wiki/Solvency "Solvency") and declines in credit availability led to plummeting stock and [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity") prices in late 2008 and early 2009.[[26]](#cite_note-26) The crisis rapidly spread into a global economic shock, resulting in several [bank failures](/wiki/Bank_failure "Bank failure").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Economies worldwide slowed during this period since credit tightened and international trade declined.[[28]](#cite_note-28) Housing markets suffered and unemployment soared, resulting in [evictions](/wiki/Eviction "Eviction") and [foreclosures](/wiki/Foreclosure "Foreclosure"). Several businesses failed.[[29]](#cite_note-gofromhere-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) From its peak in the second quarter of 2007 at $61.4 trillion, household wealth in the United States fell $11 trillion, to $50.4 trillion by the end of the first quarter of 2009, resulting in a decline in consumption, then a decline in business investment.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-greatcrash-32) In the fourth quarter of 2008, the quarter-over-quarter decline in real GDP in the U.S. was 8.4%.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The U.S. unemployment rate peaked at 11.0% in October 2009, the highest rate since 1983 and roughly twice the pre-crisis rate. The average hours per work week declined to 33, the lowest level since the government began collecting the data in 1964.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The economic crisis started in the U.S. but spread to the rest of the world.[[29]](#cite_note-gofromhere-29) U.S. consumption accounted for more than a third of the growth in global consumption between 2000 and 2007 and the rest of the world depended on the U.S. consumer as a source of demand.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) Toxic securities were owned by corporate and institutional investors globally. Derivatives such as [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap") also increased the linkage between large financial institutions. The [de-leveraging](/wiki/De-leveraging "De-leveraging") of financial institutions, as assets were sold to pay back obligations that could not be refinanced in frozen credit markets, further accelerated the solvency crisis and caused a decrease in international trade. Reductions in the growth rates of [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_countries "Developing countries") were due to falls in trade, commodity prices, investment and [remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") sent from migrant workers (example: Armenia[[38]](#cite_note-38)). States with fragile political systems feared that investors from Western states would withdraw their money because of the crisis.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

As part of [national fiscal policy response to the Great Recession](/wiki/National_fiscal_policy_response_to_the_Great_Recession "National fiscal policy response to the Great Recession"), governments and central banks, including the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve"), the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") and the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), provided then-unprecedented trillions of dollars in [bailouts](/wiki/Bailout "Bailout") and [stimulus](/wiki/Stimulus_(economics) "Stimulus (economics)"), including expansive [fiscal policy](/wiki/Fiscal_policy "Fiscal policy") and [monetary policy](/wiki/Monetary_policy "Monetary policy") to offset the decline in consumption and lending capacity, avoid a further collapse, encourage lending, restore faith in the integral [commercial paper](/wiki/Commercial_paper "Commercial paper") markets, avoid the risk of a [deflationary spiral](/wiki/Deflationary_spiral "Deflationary spiral"), and provide banks with enough funds to allow customers to make withdrawals.[[40]](#cite_note-40) In effect, the central banks went from being the "[lender of last resort](/wiki/Lender_of_last_resort "Lender of last resort")" to the "lender of only resort" for a significant portion of the economy. In some cases the Fed was considered the "buyer of last resort".[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45) During the fourth quarter of 2008, these central banks purchased US$2.5 trillion (~$3.57 trillion in 2024) of government debt and troubled private assets from banks. This was the largest liquidity injection into the credit market, and the largest monetary policy action in world history. Following a model initiated by the [2008 United Kingdom bank rescue package](/wiki/2008_United_Kingdom_bank_rescue_package "2008 United Kingdom bank rescue package"),[[46]](#cite_note-Langley2015-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47) the governments of European nations and the United States guaranteed the debt issued by their banks and raised the capital of their national banking systems, ultimately purchasing $1.5 trillion newly issued [preferred stock](/wiki/Preferred_stock "Preferred stock") in major banks.[[32]](#cite_note-greatcrash-32) The [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") created then-significant amounts of new currency as a method to combat the [liquidity trap](/wiki/Liquidity_trap "Liquidity trap").[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Bailouts came in the form of trillions of dollars of loans, asset purchases, guarantees, and direct spending.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Significant controversy accompanied the bailouts, such as in the case of the [AIG bonus payments controversy](/wiki/AIG_bonus_payments_controversy "AIG bonus payments controversy"), leading to the development of a variety of "decision making frameworks", to help balance competing policy interests during times of financial crisis.[[50]](#cite_note-50) [Alistair Darling](/wiki/Alistair_Darling "Alistair Darling"), the U.K.'s [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer") at the time of the crisis, stated in 2018 that Britain came within hours of "a breakdown of law and order" the day that [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") was bailed-out.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Instead of financing more domestic loans, some banks instead spent some of the stimulus money in more profitable areas such as investing in emerging markets and foreign currencies.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

In July 2010, the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") was enacted in the United States to "promote the financial stability of the United States".[[53]](#cite_note-53) The [Basel III](/wiki/Basel_III "Basel III") capital and liquidity standards were adopted worldwide.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Since the 2008 financial crisis, consumer regulators in America have more closely supervised sellers of credit cards and home mortgages in order to deter anticompetitive practices that led to the crisis.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

At least two major reports on the causes of the crisis were produced by the U.S. Congress: the [Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission](/wiki/Financial_Crisis_Inquiry_Commission "Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission") report, released January 2011, and a report by the [United States Senate Homeland Security Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Homeland_Security_Permanent_Subcommittee_on_Investigations "United States Senate Homeland Security Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations") entitled [Wall Street and the Financial Crisis: Anatomy of a Financial Collapse](/wiki/Wall_Street_and_the_Financial_Crisis:_Anatomy_of_a_Financial_Collapse "Wall Street and the Financial Crisis: Anatomy of a Financial Collapse"), released April 2011.

In total, 47 bankers served jail time as a result of the crisis, over half of whom were from Iceland, where the crisis was the most severe and led to the collapse of all three major Icelandic banks.[[56]](#cite_note-jail-56) In April 2012, [Geir Haarde](/wiki/Geir_Haarde "Geir Haarde") of Iceland became the only politician to be convicted as a result of the crisis.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Only one banker in the United States served jail time as a result of the crisis, [Kareem Serageldin](/wiki/Kareem_Serageldin "Kareem Serageldin"), a banker at [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse") who was sentenced to 30 months in jail and returned $24.6 million in compensation for manipulating bond prices to hide $1 billion of losses.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[56]](#cite_note-jail-56) No individuals in the United Kingdom were convicted as a result of the crisis.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61) In [*SEC v. Goldman Sachs*](/wiki/SEC_v._Goldman_Sachs_ABACUS "SEC v. Goldman Sachs ABACUS"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") paid a $550 million civil penalty and acknowledged that its marketing materials for CDO contained incomplete information, settling [securities fraud](/wiki/Securities_fraud "Securities fraud") charges after allegedly anticipating the financial crisis and selling toxic investments to its clients.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-:12-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)

With fewer resources to risk in [creative destruction](/wiki/Creative_destruction "Creative destruction"), the number of patent applications was flat, compared to exponential increases in patent application in prior years.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/2008_Top1percentUSA.svg/500px-2008_Top1percentUSA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2008_Top1percentUSA.svg)

Share of income going to the top 1% of earners, one measure of economic inequality in the US from 1913 to 2008.

Typical American families did not fare well, nor did the "wealthy-but-not-wealthiest" families just beneath the pyramid's top.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68) However, half of the poorest families in the United States did not have wealth declines at all during the crisis because they generally did not own financial investments whose value could fluctuate. The Federal Reserve surveyed 4,000 households from 2007 to 2009, and found that the total wealth of 63% of all Americans declined in that period and 77% of the richest families had a decrease in total wealth, while only 50% of those on the bottom of the pyramid suffered a decrease.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

## Timeline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Timeline")]

See also: [Global financial crisis in September 2008](/wiki/Global_financial_crisis_in_September_2008 "Global financial crisis in September 2008"), [Global financial crisis in October 2008](/wiki/Global_financial_crisis_in_October_2008 "Global financial crisis in October 2008"), [Global financial crisis in November 2008](/wiki/Global_financial_crisis_in_November_2008 "Global financial crisis in November 2008"), [Global financial crisis in December 2008](/wiki/Global_financial_crisis_in_December_2008 "Global financial crisis in December 2008"), [Global financial crisis in 2009](/wiki/Global_financial_crisis_in_2009 "Global financial crisis in 2009"), [United States bear market of 2007–2009](/wiki/United_States_bear_market_of_2007%E2%80%932009 "United States bear market of 2007–2009"), [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act"), [Regulatory responses to the subprime crisis](/wiki/Regulatory_responses_to_the_subprime_crisis "Regulatory responses to the subprime crisis"), and [Subprime mortgage crisis solutions debate](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis_solutions_debate "Subprime mortgage crisis solutions debate")

The following is a timeline of the major events of the financial crisis, including government responses, and the subsequent economic recovery.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Pre-2007

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pre-2007")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Cost_of_housing_by_State.webp/500px-Cost_of_housing_by_State.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Cost_of_housing_by_State.webp)

Cost of housing by state from the year 2000 to 2022

* April 28, 2004: Meeting is held in the basement of the Federal Reserve with the Gang of Five (Goldman Sachs, Lehman Brothers, Merrill Lynch, Bear Stearns and Morgan Stanley) and the five members of the Securities and Exchange Commission to create an exemption for the five largest investment banks in the U.S. from having to adhere to the [Net Capital Rule](/wiki/Net_capital_rule "Net capital rule").[[76]](#cite_note-76)
* May 19, 2005: Fund manager [Michael Burry](/wiki/Michael_Burry "Michael Burry") closed a [credit default swap](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap") against subprime mortgage bonds with [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank") valued at $60 million – the first such CDS. He projected they would become volatile within two years of the low "teaser rate" of the mortgages expiring.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)
* 2006: After years of above-average price increases, housing prices peaked and [mortgage loan](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan") delinquency rose—the first sign of the bursting of the [United States housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble").[[79]](#cite_note-aspnewhome-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Homeownership-80) Due to increasingly lax underwriting standards, one-third of all mortgages in 2006 were subprime or no-documentation loans,[[81]](#cite_note-81) which comprised 17% of home purchases that year.[[82]](#cite_note-newcentury-82)
* May 2006: [JPMorgan](/wiki/JPMorgan "JPMorgan") warned clients of a housing downturn, especially in sub-prime housing.[[83]](#cite_note-ii2007-83)
* August 2006: The [yield curve](/wiki/Yield_curve "Yield curve") inverted, signaling a recession was likely within a year or two.[[84]](#cite_note-84)
* November 2006: [UBS](/wiki/UBS "UBS") warned of "an impending crisis in the U.S. housing market".[[83]](#cite_note-ii2007-83)

### 2007 (January–August)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2007 (January–August)")]

* February 27, 2007: Stock prices in China and the U.S. fell by the most since 2003 as reports of a decline in home prices and [durable goods](/wiki/Durable_good "Durable good") orders stoked growth fears, with [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan") predicting a recession.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Due to increased delinquency rates in [subprime lending](/wiki/Subprime_lending "Subprime lending"), [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac") said that it would stop investing in certain subprime loans.[[86]](#cite_note-86)
* April 2, 2007: [New Century](/wiki/New_Century "New Century"), an American [real estate investment trust](/wiki/Real_estate_investment_trust "Real estate investment trust") specializing in [subprime lending](/wiki/Subprime_lending "Subprime lending") and [securitization](/wiki/Securitization "Securitization"), filed for [Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection](/wiki/Chapter_11_bankruptcy_protection "Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection"). This propagated the [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis").[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[82]](#cite_note-newcentury-82)[[89]](#cite_note-usatodaytimeline-89)[[90]](#cite_note-BBC_Timeline-90)
* June 20, 2007: After receiving [margin calls](/wiki/Margin_call "Margin call"), [Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns") bailed out two of its hedge funds with $20 billion of exposure to [collateralized debt obligations](/wiki/Collateralized_debt_obligation "Collateralized debt obligation") including subprime mortgages.[[91]](#cite_note-91)
* July 19, 2007: [The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA)](/wiki/Dow_Jones_Industrial_Average "Dow Jones Industrial Average") closed above 14,000 for the first time, at 14,000.41.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92)
* July 30, 2007: [IKB Deutsche Industriebank](/wiki/IKB_Deutsche_Industriebank "IKB Deutsche Industriebank"), the first banking casualty of the crisis, announces its bailout by German public financial institution [KfW](/wiki/KfW "KfW").[[93]](#cite_note-93)
* July 31, 2007: [Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns") liquidated the two hedge funds.[[89]](#cite_note-usatodaytimeline-89)
* August 6, 2007: [American Home Mortgage](/wiki/American_Home_Mortgage "American Home Mortgage") filed for bankruptcy.[[89]](#cite_note-usatodaytimeline-89)
* August 9, 2007: [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas") blocked withdrawals from three of its [hedge funds](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") with a total of $2.2 billion in [assets under management](/wiki/Assets_under_management "Assets under management"), due to "a complete evaporation of liquidity", making valuation of the funds impossible – a clear sign that banks were refusing to do business with each other.[[90]](#cite_note-BBC_Timeline-90)[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)
* August 16, 2007: The DJIA closes at 12,945.78 after falling on 12 out of the previous 20 trading days following its peak. It had fallen 1,164.63 or 8.3%.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92)

### 2007 (September–December)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2007 (September–December)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg/250px-Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Northern_Rock_Queue.jpg)

People queueing outside a [Northern Rock](/wiki/Northern_Rock "Northern Rock") branch in the United Kingdom to withdraw their savings during the financial crisis

* September 14, 2007: [Northern Rock](/wiki/Northern_Rock "Northern Rock"), a medium-sized and highly [leveraged](/wiki/Leveraged "Leveraged") British bank, received support from the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England").[[96]](#cite_note-96) This led to investor panic and a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run").[[97]](#cite_note-northernrock-97)
* September 18, 2007: The [Federal Open Market Committee](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee") began reducing the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") from its peak of 5.25% in response to worries about liquidity and confidence.[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)
* September 28, 2007: [NetBank](/wiki/NetBank "NetBank") suffered from [bank failure](/wiki/Bank_failure "Bank failure") and filed bankruptcy due to exposure to home loans.[[100]](#cite_note-100)
* October 9, 2007: The DJIA hit its peak closing price of 14,164.53.[[101]](#cite_note-101)
* October 15, 2007: [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), and [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") announced plans for the $80 billion [Master Liquidity Enhancement Conduit](/wiki/Master_Liquidity_Enhancement_Conduit "Master Liquidity Enhancement Conduit") to provide liquidity to [structured investment vehicles](/wiki/Structured_investment_vehicle "Structured investment vehicle"). The plan was abandoned in December.[[102]](#cite_note-102)
* November 26, 2007: US markets enter a correction as worries about the financial sector continued to mount.[[103]](#cite_note-103)
* December 2007: Unemployment in the US hit 5%.[[104]](#cite_note-:6-104)
* December 12, 2007: The [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") instituted the [Term auction facility](/wiki/Term_auction_facility "Term auction facility") to supply short-term credit to banks with sub-prime mortgages.[[105]](#cite_note-105)
* December 17, 2007: [Delta Financial Corporation](/wiki/Delta_Financial_Corporation "Delta Financial Corporation") filed bankruptcy after failing to [securitize](/wiki/Securitize "Securitize") subprime loans.[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* December 19, 2007: the [Standard and Poor's](/wiki/Standard_and_Poor%27s "Standard and Poor's") rating agency downgrades the ratings of many monoline insurers which pay out bonds that fail.[[107]](#cite_note-107)
* December 31, 2007: Despite volatility through the last part of the year, markets close above where they started the year, with the DJIA closing at 13,264.82, up 6.4% for the year.[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### 2008 (January–August)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2008 (January–August)")]

* January 11, 2008: [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America") agreed to buy [Countrywide Financial](/wiki/Countrywide_Financial "Countrywide Financial") for $4 billion in stock.[[109]](#cite_note-109)
* January 18, 2008: Stock markets fell to a yearly low as the credit rating of [Ambac](/wiki/Ambac "Ambac"), a [bond insurance](/wiki/Bond_insurance "Bond insurance") company, was downgraded. Meanwhile, an increase in the amount of withdrawals caused Scottish Equitable to implement delays of up to 12 months on people wishing to withdraw money.[[110]](#cite_note-110)
* January 21, 2008: As US markets were closed for [Martin Luther King Jr. Day](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr._Day "Martin Luther King Jr. Day"), the [FTSE 100 Index](/wiki/FTSE_100_Index "FTSE 100 Index") in the United Kingdom tumbled 323.5 points or 5.5% in its largest crash since the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks").[[111]](#cite_note-:7-111)
* January 22, 2008: The US Federal Reserve cut interest rates by 0.75% to stimulate the economy, the largest drop in 25 years and the first emergency cut since 2001.[[111]](#cite_note-:7-111)
* January 2008: U.S. stocks had the worst January since 2000 over concerns about the exposure of companies that issue [bond insurance](/wiki/Bond_insurance "Bond insurance").[[112]](#cite_note-112)
* February 13, 2008: The [Economic Stimulus Act of 2008](/wiki/Economic_Stimulus_Act_of_2008 "Economic Stimulus Act of 2008") was enacted, which included a tax rebate.[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)
* February 22, 2008: The [nationalisation of Northern Rock](/wiki/Nationalisation_of_Northern_Rock "Nationalisation of Northern Rock") was completed.[[97]](#cite_note-northernrock-97)
* March 5, 2008: [The Carlyle Group](/wiki/The_Carlyle_Group "The Carlyle Group") received [margin calls](/wiki/Margin_calls "Margin calls") on its mortgage bond fund.[[115]](#cite_note-115)
* March 17, 2008: [Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns"), with $46 billion of [mortgage](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage") assets that had not been written down and $10 trillion in total assets, faced bankruptcy; instead, in its first emergency meeting in 30 years, the Federal Reserve agreed to guarantee its bad loans to facilitate its acquisition by [JPMorgan Chase](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase "JPMorgan Chase") for $2/share. A week earlier, the stock was trading at $60/share and a year earlier it traded for $178/share. The buyout price was increased to $10/share the following week.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)
* March 18, 2008: In a contentious meeting, the Federal Reserve cut the [federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") by 75 basis points, its 6th cut in 6 months.[[119]](#cite_note-119) It also allowed [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae") & [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac") to buy $200 billion in subprime mortgages from banks. Officials thought this would contain the possible crisis. The [U.S. dollar](/wiki/U.S._dollar "U.S. dollar") weakened and commodity prices soared.[*[data missing](/wiki/Wikipedia:Cleanup "Wikipedia:Cleanup")*][[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)[[122]](#cite_note-122)
* Late June 2008: Despite the U.S. stock market falling to 20% below its highs, commodity-related stocks soared as oil traded above $140/barrel for the first time and steel prices rose above $1,000 per ton. Worries about [inflation](/wiki/Inflation "Inflation") combined with strong demand from China encouraged people to invest in [commodities](/wiki/Commodities "Commodities") during the [2000s commodities boom](/wiki/2000s_commodities_boom "2000s commodities boom").[[123]](#cite_note-123)[[124]](#cite_note-124)
* July 11, 2008: [IndyMac](/wiki/IndyMac "IndyMac") failed. Oil prices peaked at $147.50[[125]](#cite_note-indymactaken-125)[[111]](#cite_note-:7-111)
* July 30, 2008: The [Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008](/wiki/Housing_and_Economic_Recovery_Act_of_2008 "Housing and Economic Recovery Act of 2008") was enacted.[[126]](#cite_note-126)
* August 2008: Unemployment hit 6% in the US.[[104]](#cite_note-:6-104)

### 2008 (September)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2008 (September)")]

* September 7, 2008: The [Federal takeover of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac](/wiki/Federal_takeover_of_Fannie_Mae_and_Freddie_Mac "Federal takeover of Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac") was implemented.[[127]](#cite_note-127)
* September 15, 2008: After the Federal Reserve declined to guarantee its loans as it did for Bear Stearns, the [bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Bankruptcy_of_Lehman_Brothers "Bankruptcy of Lehman Brothers") led to a 504.48-point (4.42%) drop in the DJIA, its worst decline in seven years. To avoid bankruptcy, [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch") was acquired by [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America") for $50 billion in a transaction facilitated by the government.[[128]](#cite_note-128) Lehman had been in talks to be sold to either Bank of America or [Barclays](/wiki/Barclays "Barclays") but neither bank wanted to acquire the entire company.[[129]](#cite_note-129)
* September 16, 2008: The [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") took over [American International Group](/wiki/American_International_Group "American International Group") with $85 billion in debt and equity funding. The [Reserve Primary Fund](/wiki/Reserve_Primary_Fund "Reserve Primary Fund") "[broke the buck](/wiki/Break_the_buck "Break the buck")" as a result of its exposure to [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers") securities.[[130]](#cite_note-130)
* September 17, 2008: Investors withdrew $144 billion from U.S. [money market funds](/wiki/Money_market_fund "Money market fund"), the equivalent of a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") on [money market funds](/wiki/Money_market_fund "Money market fund"), which frequently invest in [commercial paper](/wiki/Commercial_paper "Commercial paper") issued by corporations to fund their operations and payrolls, causing the short-term lending market to freeze. The withdrawal compared to $7.1 billion in withdrawals the week prior. This interrupted the ability of corporations to rollover their [short-term debt](/wiki/Short-term_debt "Short-term debt"). The U.S. government extended insurance for money market accounts analogous to bank [deposit insurance](/wiki/Deposit_insurance "Deposit insurance") via a temporary guarantee[[131]](#cite_note-131) and with Federal Reserve programs to purchase commercial paper.
* September 18, 2008: In a dramatic meeting, [United States Secretary of the Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Secretary_of_the_Treasury "United States Secretary of the Treasury") [Henry Paulson](/wiki/Henry_Paulson "Henry Paulson") and [Chair of the Federal Reserve](/wiki/Chair_of_the_Federal_Reserve "Chair of the Federal Reserve") [Ben Bernanke](/wiki/Ben_Bernanke "Ben Bernanke") met with [Speaker of the United States House of Representatives](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi") and warned that the credit markets were close to a complete meltdown. Bernanke requested a $700 billion fund to acquire toxic mortgages and reportedly told them: "If we don't do this, we may not have an economy on Monday".[[132]](#cite_note-132)
* September 19, 2008: The Federal Reserve created the [Asset Backed Commercial Paper Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility](/wiki/Asset_Backed_Commercial_Paper_Money_Market_Mutual_Fund_Liquidity_Facility "Asset Backed Commercial Paper Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility") to temporarily insure money market funds and allow the credit markets to continue operating.[[133]](#cite_note-133)
* September 20, 2008: Paulson requested the U.S. Congress authorize a $700 billion fund to acquire toxic mortgages, telling Congress "If it doesn't pass, then heaven help us all".[[134]](#cite_note-134)
* September 21, 2008: [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") and [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley") converted from [investment banks](/wiki/Investment_banks "Investment banks") to [bank holding companies](/wiki/Bank_holding_companies "Bank holding companies") to increase their protection by the Federal Reserve.[[135]](#cite_note-135)[[136]](#cite_note-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137)[[138]](#cite_note-138)
* September 22, 2008: [MUFG Bank](/wiki/MUFG_Bank "MUFG Bank") acquired 20% of [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley").[[139]](#cite_note-139)
* September 23, 2008: [Berkshire Hathaway](/wiki/Berkshire_Hathaway "Berkshire Hathaway") made a $5 billion investment in [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs").[[140]](#cite_note-140)
* September 26, 2008: [Washington Mutual](/wiki/Washington_Mutual "Washington Mutual") went bankrupt and was seized by the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") after a [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") in which panicked depositors withdrew $16.7 billion in 10 days.[[141]](#cite_note-141)
* September 29, 2008: By a vote of 225–208, with most Democrats in support and Republicans against, the House of Representatives rejected the [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act_of_2008 "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008"), which included the $700 billion [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program"). In response, the DJIA dropped 777.68 points, or 6.98%, then the largest point drop in history. The S&P 500 Index fell 8.8% and the Nasdaq Composite fell 9.1%.[[142]](#cite_note-142) Several stock market indices worldwide fell 10%. Gold prices soared to $900/ounce. The Federal Reserve doubled its credit swaps with foreign central banks as they all needed to provide liquidity. [Wachovia](/wiki/Wachovia "Wachovia") reached a deal to sell itself to Citigroup; however, the deal would have made shares worthless and required government funding.[[143]](#cite_note-143)
* September 30, 2008: President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") addressed the country, saying "Congress must act. ... Our economy is depending on decisive action from the government. The sooner we address the problem, the sooner we can get back on the path of growth and job creation". The DJIA rebounded 4.7%.[[144]](#cite_note-144)

### 2008 (October)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2008 (October)")]

* October 1, 2008: The U.S. Senate passed the [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act_of_2008 "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008").[[145]](#cite_note-145)
* October 2, 2008: Stock market indices fell 4% as investors were nervous ahead of a vote in the U.S. House of Representatives on the [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act_of_2008 "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008").[[146]](#cite_note-146)
* October 3, 2008: The House of Representatives passed the [Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008](/wiki/Emergency_Economic_Stabilization_Act_of_2008 "Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008") and the $700 billion Troubled Asset Relief Program.[[147]](#cite_note-147) Bush signed the legislation that same day.[[148]](#cite_note-148) Wachovia reached a deal to be acquired by [Wells Fargo](/wiki/Wells_Fargo "Wells Fargo") in a deal that did not require government funding.[[149]](#cite_note-149)
* October 6–10, 2008: From October 6–10, 2008, the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) closed lower in all five sessions. Volume levels were record-breaking. The DJIA fell 1,874.19 points, or 18.2%, in its worst weekly decline ever on both a points and percentage basis. The S&P 500 fell more than 20%.[[150]](#cite_note-150)
* October 7, 2008: In the U.S., per the Emergency Economic Stabilization Act of 2008, the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") increased deposit insurance coverage to $250,000 per depositor.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/S%26P_BSE_SENSEX_chart.svg/500px-S%26P_BSE_SENSEX_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:S%26P_BSE_SENSEX_chart.svg)

During the 2008 global financial crisis, the [BSE SENSEX](/wiki/BSE_SENSEX "BSE SENSEX") experienced a sharp decline. It dropped from over 21,000 points in January 2008 to below 8,000 points in October 2008.[[152]](#cite_note-152)

* October 8, 2008: The [Indonesian stock market](/wiki/Indonesia_Stock_Exchange "Indonesia Stock Exchange") halted trading after a 10% drop in one day.[[153]](#cite_note-153) Global central banks held emergency meetings and coordinated interest rate cuts before the US stock markets opened.[[154]](#cite_note-154)
* October 11, 2008: The head of the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") (IMF) warned that the world financial system was teetering on the "brink of systemic meltdown".[[155]](#cite_note-155)
* October 14, 2008: Having been suspended for three successive trading days (October 9, 10 and 13), the Icelandic stock market reopened on October 14, with the main index, the [OMX Iceland 15](/wiki/OMX_Iceland_15 "OMX Iceland 15"), closing at 678.4, which was about 77% lower than the 3,004.6 at the close on October 8, after the value of the three big banks, which had formed 73.2% of the value of the OMX Iceland 15, had been set to zero, leading to the [2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis](/wiki/2008%E2%80%932011_Icelandic_financial_crisis "2008–2011 Icelandic financial crisis").[[156]](#cite_note-156) The [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") created the [Temporary Liquidity Guarantee Program](/wiki/Temporary_Liquidity_Guarantee_Program "Temporary Liquidity Guarantee Program") to guarantee the senior debt of all FDIC-insured institutions through June 30, 2009.[[157]](#cite_note-157)
* October 16, 2008: A rescue plan was unveiled for Swiss banks [UBS AG](/wiki/UBS_AG "UBS AG") and [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse").[[158]](#cite_note-158)
* October 24, 2008: Many of the world's stock exchanges experienced the worst declines in their history, with drops of around 10% in most indices.[[159]](#cite_note-159) In the U.S., the DJIA fell 3.6%, although not as much as other markets.[[160]](#cite_note-160) The [United States dollar](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar"), the [Japanese yen](/wiki/Japanese_yen "Japanese yen") and the [Swiss franc](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc") soared against other major currencies, particularly the [British pound](/wiki/British_pound "British pound") and [Canadian dollar](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar"), as world investors sought safe havens. A [currency crisis](/wiki/Currency_crisis "Currency crisis") developed, with investors transferring vast capital resources into stronger currencies, leading many governments of emerging economies to seek aid from the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").[[161]](#cite_note-161)[[162]](#cite_note-162) Later that day, the deputy governor of the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England"), [Charlie Bean](/wiki/Charlie_Bean_(economist) "Charlie Bean (economist)"), suggested that "This is a once in a lifetime crisis, and possibly the largest financial crisis of its kind in human history".[[163]](#cite_note-163) In a transaction pushed by regulators, [PNC Financial Services](/wiki/PNC_Financial_Services "PNC Financial Services") agreed to acquire [National City Corp.](/wiki/National_City_Corp. "National City Corp.")[[164]](#cite_note-164)

### 2008 (November–December)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2008 (November–December)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/22_2007-2008_global_financial_crisis_effect_in_Bristol_UK_-_credit_crunch_lunch.jpg/250px-22_2007-2008_global_financial_crisis_effect_in_Bristol_UK_-_credit_crunch_lunch.jpg)](/wiki/File:22_2007-2008_global_financial_crisis_effect_in_Bristol_UK_-_credit_crunch_lunch.jpg)

Restaurant in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), United Kingdom, advertising cheap "Credit Crunch Lunch"

* November 6, 2008: The IMF predicted a worldwide recession of −0.3% for 2009. On the same day, the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") and the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), respectively, reduced their interest rates from 4.5% to 3%, and from 3.75% to 3.25%.[[165]](#cite_note-165)
* November 10, 2008: [American Express](/wiki/American_Express "American Express") converted to a [bank holding company](/wiki/Bank_holding_company "Bank holding company").[[166]](#cite_note-166)
* November 20, 2008: Iceland obtained an emergency loan from the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund") after the failure of banks in Iceland resulted in a devaluation of the [Icelandic króna](/wiki/Icelandic_kr%C3%B3na "Icelandic króna") and threatened the government with bankruptcy.[[167]](#cite_note-167)
* November 25, 2008: The [Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility](/wiki/Term_Asset-Backed_Securities_Loan_Facility "Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility") was announced.[[168]](#cite_note-168)
* November 29, 2008: Economist [Dean Baker](/wiki/Dean_Baker "Dean Baker") observed:
  > There is a really good reason for tighter credit. Tens of millions of homeowners who had substantial equity in their homes two years ago have little or nothing today. Businesses are facing the worst downturn since the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). This matters for credit decisions. A homeowner with equity in her home is very unlikely to default on a car loan or credit card debt. They will draw on this equity rather than lose their car and/or have a default placed on their credit record. On the other hand, a homeowner who has no equity is a serious default risk. In the case of businesses, their creditworthiness depends on their future profits. Profit prospects look much worse in November 2008 than they did in November 2007 ... While many banks are obviously at the brink, consumers and businesses would be facing a much harder time getting credit right now even if the financial system were rock solid. The problem with the economy is the loss of close to $6 trillion in housing wealth and an even larger amount of stock wealth.[[169]](#cite_note-169)
* December 1, 2008: The NBER announced the US was in a recession and had been since December 2007. The Dow tumbled 679.95 points or 7.8% on the news.[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[92]](#cite_note-:5-92)
* December 6, 2008: The [2008 Greek riots](/wiki/2008_Greek_riots "2008 Greek riots") began, sparked in part by economic conditions in the country.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* December 16, 2008: The federal funds rate was lowered to 0%.[[171]](#cite_note-171)
* December 20, 2008: Financing under the [Troubled Asset Relief Program](/wiki/Troubled_Asset_Relief_Program "Troubled Asset Relief Program") was made available to [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") and [Chrysler](/wiki/Chrysler "Chrysler").[[172]](#cite_note-172)

### 2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2009")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Payroll_tax_history.jpg/500px-Payroll_tax_history.jpg)](/wiki/File:Payroll_tax_history.jpg)

The [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") provided a [payroll tax](/wiki/Payroll_tax "Payroll tax") credit repealed in late 2010.

* January 6, 2009: Citi claimed that Singapore would experience "the most severe recession in [its] history" in 2009. In the end, the economy grew in 2009 by 0.1% and in 2010 by 14.5%.[[173]](#cite_note-:62-173)[[174]](#cite_note-174)[[175]](#cite_note-175)
* January 20–26, 2009: The [2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests](/wiki/2009_Icelandic_financial_crisis_protests "2009 Icelandic financial crisis protests") intensified and the Icelandic government collapsed.[[176]](#cite_note-176)
* February 13, 2009: Congress approved the [American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009"), a $787 billion economic stimulus package. President Barack Obama signed it February 17.[[177]](#cite_note-177)[[178]](#cite_note-arraecon-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179)[[180]](#cite_note-180)
* February 20, 2009: The DJIA closed at a six-year low, amidst worries that the largest banks in the United States would have to be [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalized "Nationalized").[[181]](#cite_note-181)
* February 27, 2009: The [DJIA](/wiki/DJIA "DJIA") closed at its lowest value since 1997 as the U.S. government increased its stake in [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup") to 36%, raising further fears of nationalization and a report showed that GDP shrank at the sharpest pace in 26 years.[[182]](#cite_note-182)
* Early March 2009: The drop in stock prices was compared to that of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression").[[183]](#cite_note-183)[[184]](#cite_note-184)
* March 3, 2009: President Obama stated that "Buying stocks is a potentially good deal if you've got a long-term perspective on it".[[185]](#cite_note-185)
* March 6, 2009: The Dow Jones hit its lowest level of 6,469.95, a drop of 54% from its peak of 14,164 on October 9, 2007, over a span of 17 months, before beginning to recover.[[186]](#cite_note-186)
* March 10, 2009: Shares of [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup") rose 38% after the CEO said that the company was profitable in the first two months of the year and expressed optimism about its capital position going forward. Major stock market indices rose 5–7%, marking the bottom of the stock market decline.[[187]](#cite_note-187)
* March 12, 2009: Stock market indices in the U.S. rose another 4% after [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America") said it was profitable in January and February and would likely not need more government funding. [Bernie Madoff](/wiki/Bernie_Madoff "Bernie Madoff") was convicted.[[188]](#cite_note-188)
* First quarter of 2009: For the first quarter of 2009, the annualized rate of decline in GDP was 14.4% in Germany, 15.2% in Japan, 7.4% in the UK, 18% in Latvia,[[189]](#cite_note-189) 9.8% in the Euro area and 21.5% for Mexico.[[29]](#cite_note-gofromhere-29)
* April 2, 2009: Unrest over [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_policy "Economic policy") and bonuses paid to bankers resulted in the [2009 G20 London summit protests](/wiki/2009_G20_London_summit_protests "2009 G20 London summit protests").
* April 10, 2009: *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine declared "More Quickly Than It Began, The Banking Crisis Is Over".[[190]](#cite_note-190)
* April 29, 2009: The Federal Reserve projected GDP growth of 2.5–3% in 2010; an unemployment plateau in 2009 and 2010 around 10% with moderation in 2011; and inflation rates around 1–2%.[[191]](#cite_note-191)
* May 1, 2009: People protested economic conditions globally during the [2009 May Day protests](/wiki/2009_May_Day_protests "2009 May Day protests").
* May 20, 2009: President Obama signed the [Fraud Enforcement and Recovery Act of 2009](/wiki/Fraud_Enforcement_and_Recovery_Act_of_2009 "Fraud Enforcement and Recovery Act of 2009").
* June 2009: The [National Bureau of Economic Research](/wiki/National_Bureau_of_Economic_Research "National Bureau of Economic Research") (NBER) declared June 2009 as the end date of the U.S. recession.[[192]](#cite_note-192) The [Federal Open Market Committee](/wiki/Federal_Open_Market_Committee "Federal Open Market Committee") release in June 2009 stated:
  > ... the pace of economic contraction is slowing. Conditions in financial markets have generally improved in recent months. Household spending has shown further signs of stabilizing but remains constrained by ongoing job losses, lower housing wealth, and tight credit. Businesses are cutting back on fixed investment and staffing but appear to be making progress in bringing inventory stocks into better alignment with sales. Although economic activity is likely to remain weak for a time, the Committee continues to anticipate that policy actions to stabilize financial markets and institutions, fiscal and monetary stimulus, and market forces will contribute to a gradual resumption of sustainable [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") in a context of price stability.[[193]](#cite_note-193)
* June 17, 2009: [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") and key advisers introduced a series of regulatory proposals that addressed [consumer protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection"), [executive pay](/wiki/Executive_pay "Executive pay"), bank capital requirements, expanded regulation of the [shadow banking system](/wiki/Shadow_banking_system "Shadow banking system") and [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)"), and enhanced authority for the Federal Reserve to safely wind down systemically important institutions.[[194]](#cite_note-194)[[195]](#cite_note-195)[[196]](#cite_note-196)
* December 11, 2009: [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") passed bill H.R. 4173, a precursor to what became the [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act").[[197]](#cite_note-197)

### 2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2010")]

* January 22, 2010: President Obama introduced "The [Volcker Rule](/wiki/Volcker_Rule "Volcker Rule")" limiting the ability of banks to engage in [proprietary trading](/wiki/Proprietary_trading "Proprietary trading"), named after [Paul Volcker](/wiki/Paul_Volcker "Paul Volcker"), who publicly argued for the proposed changes.[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199) Obama also proposed a [Financial Crisis Responsibility Fee](/wiki/Financial_Crisis_Responsibility_Fee "Financial Crisis Responsibility Fee") on large banks.
* January 27, 2010: President Obama declared on "the markets are now stabilized, and we've recovered most of the money we spent on the banks".[[200]](#cite_note-200)
* First quarter 2010: Delinquency rates in the United States peaked at 11.54%.[[201]](#cite_note-201)
* April 15, 2010: U.S. Senate introduced bill S.3217, [Restoring American Financial Stability Act of 2010](/wiki/Restoring_American_Financial_Stability_Act_of_2010 "Restoring American Financial Stability Act of 2010").[[202]](#cite_note-202)
* May 2010: The U.S. Senate passed the [Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd-Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act"). The Volcker Rule against proprietary trading was not part of the legislation.[[203]](#cite_note-203)
* July 21, 2010: [Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act](/wiki/Dodd%E2%80%93Frank_Wall_Street_Reform_and_Consumer_Protection_Act "Dodd–Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act") enacted.[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205)
* September 12, 2010: European regulators introduced [Basel III](/wiki/Basel_III "Basel III") regulations for banks, which increased capital ratios, limits on leverage, narrowed the definition of capital to exclude subordinated debt, limited counter-party risk, and added liquidity requirements.[[206]](#cite_note-206)[[207]](#cite_note-207) Critics argued that Basel III didn't address the problem of faulty risk-weightings. Major banks suffered losses from AAA-rated created by [financial engineering](/wiki/Financial_engineering "Financial engineering") (which creates apparently risk-free assets out of high risk collateral) that required less capital according to Basel II. Lending to AA-rated sovereigns has a risk-weight of zero, thus increasing lending to governments and leading to the next crisis.[[208]](#cite_note-208) [Johan Norberg](/wiki/Johan_Norberg "Johan Norberg") argued that regulations (Basel III among others) have indeed led to excessive lending to risky governments (see [Euro area crisis](/wiki/Euro_area_crisis "Euro area crisis")) and the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") pursues even more lending as the solution.[[209]](#cite_note-209)
* November 3, 2010: To improve economic growth, the Federal Reserve announced another round of [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing"), dubbed QE2, which included the purchase of $600 billion in long-term [Treasuries](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security") over the following eight months.[[210]](#cite_note-210)

### Post-2010

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Post-2010")]

* March 2011: Two years after the [nadir](/wiki/Nadir "Nadir") of the crisis, many stock market indices were 75% above their lows set in March 2009. Nevertheless, the lack of fundamental changes in banking and financial markets worried many market participants, including the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund").[[211]](#cite_note-Over-211)
* 2011: Median household wealth fell 35% in the U.S., from $106,591 to $68,839 between 2005 and 2011.[[212]](#cite_note-Kurtzleben-212)
* May 2012: The [Manhattan District Attorney](/wiki/Manhattan_District_Attorney "Manhattan District Attorney") indicted [Abacus Federal Savings Bank](/wiki/Abacus_Federal_Savings_Bank "Abacus Federal Savings Bank") and 19 employees for selling fraudulent mortgages to [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae"). The bank was acquitted in 2015. Abacus was the only bank prosecuted for misbehavior that precipitated the crisis.
* July 26, 2012: During the [European debt crisis](/wiki/European_debt_crisis "European debt crisis"), [President of the European Central Bank](/wiki/President_of_the_European_Central_Bank "President of the European Central Bank") [Mario Draghi](/wiki/Mario_Draghi "Mario Draghi") announced that "The ECB is ready to do whatever it takes to preserve the euro".[[213]](#cite_note-213)
* August 2012: In the United States, many homeowners still faced foreclosure and could not refinance or modify their mortgages. Foreclosure rates remained high.[[214]](#cite_note-214)
* September 13, 2012: To improve lower interest rates, support mortgage markets, and make financial conditions more accommodative, the Federal Reserve announced another round of [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing"), dubbed QE3, which included the purchase of $40 billion in long-term [Treasuries](/wiki/United_States_Treasury_security "United States Treasury security") each month.[[215]](#cite_note-215)
* 2014: A report showed that the distribution of household incomes in the United States became more unequal during the post-2008 [economic recovery](/wiki/Economic_recovery "Economic recovery"), a first for the United States but in line with the trend over the last ten economic recoveries since 1949.[[216]](#cite_note-216)[[217]](#cite_note-217) [Income inequality in the United States](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_United_States "Income inequality in the United States") grew from 2005 to 2012 in more than 2 out of 3 metropolitan areas.[[218]](#cite_note-218)
* June 2015: A study commissioned by the [ACLU](/wiki/ACLU "ACLU") found that white home-owning households recovered from the financial crisis faster than black home-owning households, widening the [racial wealth gap](/wiki/Racial_wealth_gap "Racial wealth gap") in the U.S.[[219]](#cite_note-219)
* 2017: Per the [International Monetary Fund](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund"), from 2007 to 2017, "advanced" economies accounted for only 26.5% of global [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") ([PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) growth while emerging and developing economies accounted for 73.5% of global GDP (PPP) growth.[[220]](#cite_note-220)
* August 2023: [UBS](/wiki/UBS "UBS") reaches an agreement with the [United States Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") to pay a combined $1.435 billion in civil penalties to settle a legacy matter from 2006–2007 related to the issuance, underwriting and sale of residential mortgage-backed securities.[[221]](#cite_note-221)
* In 2023, the failure of [Silicon Valley Bank](/wiki/Collapse_of_Silicon_Valley_Bank "Collapse of Silicon Valley Bank") kicked off a banking crisis in the United States, triggering a sharp decline in global bank stock prices and swift response by regulators to prevent potential global contagion. Many investors feared it would evolve into a global financial crisis. Fears of “too big to fail” US banks were reminiscent of the 2008 crisis.

## Federal actions towards the crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Federal actions towards the crisis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/HOUSING_FINANCE_REFORM-_ESSENTIAL_ELEMENTS_OF_A_GOVERNMENT_GUARANTEE_FOR_MORTGAGE-BACKED_SECURITIES_%28IA_gov.gpo.fdsys.CHRG-113shrg86598%29.pdf/page1-250px-HOUSING_FINANCE_REFORM-_ESSENTIAL_ELEMENTS_OF_A_GOVERNMENT_GUARANTEE_FOR_MORTGAGE-BACKED_SECURITIES_%28IA_gov.gpo.fdsys.CHRG-113shrg86598%29.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:HOUSING_FINANCE_REFORM-_ESSENTIAL_ELEMENTS_OF_A_GOVERNMENT_GUARANTEE_FOR_MORTGAGE-BACKED_SECURITIES_(IA_gov.gpo.fdsys.CHRG-113shrg86598).pdf)

Housing Finance Reform Bill

The expansion of central bank lending in response to the crisis was not confined to the [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve")'s provision of aid to individual financial institutions. The Federal Reserve has also conducted several innovative lending programs to improve liquidity and strengthen different financial institutions and markets, such as [Freddie Mac](/wiki/Freddie_Mac "Freddie Mac") and [Fannie Mae](/wiki/Fannie_Mae "Fannie Mae"). In this case, the major problem in the market is the lack of free cash reserves and flows to secure the loans. The Federal Reserve took many steps to deal with financial market liquidity worries. One of these steps was a credit line for major traders, who act as the Fed's partners in open market activities.[[223]](#cite_note-223) Also, loan programs were set up to make the money market mutual funds and commercial paper market more flexible. Also, the [Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility](/wiki/Term_Asset-Backed_Securities_Loan_Facility "Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility") (TALF) was put in place thanks to a joint effort with the US Department of the Treasury. This plan made it easier for consumers and businesses to get credit by giving Americans who owned high-quality asset-backed securities more credit.

Before the crisis, the Federal Reserve's stocks of Treasury securities were sold to pay for the increase in credit. This method was meant to keep banks from trying to give out their extra savings, which could cause the federal funds rate to drop below where it was supposed to be.[[224]](#cite_note-224) However, in October 2008, the Federal Reserve was granted the power to provide banks with interest payments on their surplus reserves. This created a motivation for banks to retain their reserves instead of disbursing them, thus reducing the need for the Federal Reserve to hedge its increased lending by decreases in alternative assets.[[225]](#cite_note-225)

Money market funds also went through runs when people lost faith in the market. To keep it from getting worse, the Fed said it would give money to mutual fund companies. Also, the [Department of Treasury](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Treasury "United States Department of the Treasury") said that it would briefly cover the assets of the fund. Both of these things helped get the fund market back to normal, which helped the commercial paper market, which most businesses use to run. The [FDIC](/wiki/FDIC "FDIC") also did several things, such as raising the insurance cap from $100,000 to $250,000, to boost customer trust.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg/250px-Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marriner_S._Eccles_Federal_Reserve_Board_Building.jpg)

Headquarters of the United States Federal Reserve System

They engaged in [quantitative easing](/wiki/Quantitative_easing "Quantitative easing"), which added more than $4 trillion to the financial system and got banks to start lending again, both to each other and to people. Many homeowners who were trying to keep their homes from going into default got housing credits. A package of policies was passed that let borrowers refinance their loans even though the value of their homes was less than what they still owed on their [mortgages](/wiki/Mortgage "Mortgage").[[226]](#cite_note-:03-226)

## Causes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Causes")]

See also: [Subprime crisis background information](/wiki/Subprime_crisis_background_information "Subprime crisis background information"), [Subprime crisis impact timeline](/wiki/Subprime_crisis_impact_timeline "Subprime crisis impact timeline"), [Subprime mortgage crisis solutions debate](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis_solutions_debate "Subprime mortgage crisis solutions debate"), [Indirect economic effects of the subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Indirect_economic_effects_of_the_subprime_mortgage_crisis "Indirect economic effects of the subprime mortgage crisis"), [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"), and [Inverted yield curve](/wiki/Inverted_yield_curve "Inverted yield curve")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/FFR_treasuries.webp/500px-FFR_treasuries.webp.png)](/wiki/File:FFR_treasuries.webp)

The [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve "Federal Reserve") raised the [Federal funds rate](/wiki/Federal_funds_rate "Federal funds rate") causing an [Inverted yield curve](/wiki/Inverted_yield_curve "Inverted yield curve") to slow inflation and get prices and [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity") prices down, that usually puts the economy into a [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession").

While the causes of the bubble and subsequent crash are disputed, the precipitating factor for the Financial Crisis of 2007–2008 was the bursting of the [United States housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble") and the subsequent [subprime mortgage crisis](/wiki/Subprime_mortgage_crisis "Subprime mortgage crisis"), which occurred due to a high default rate and resulting foreclosures of [mortgage loans](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan"), particularly [adjustable-rate mortgages](/wiki/Adjustable-rate_mortgage "Adjustable-rate mortgage"). Some or all of the following factors contributed to the crisis:[[227]](#cite_note-4questions-227)[[79]](#cite_note-aspnewhome-79)[[80]](#cite_note-Homeownership-80)

* In its January 2011 report, the [Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission](/wiki/Financial_Crisis_Inquiry_Commission "Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission") (FCIC, a committee of U.S. congressmen) concluded that the financial crisis was avoidable and was caused by:[[228]](#cite_note-228)[[229]](#cite_note-229)[[230]](#cite_note-230)[[231]](#cite_note-231)[[232]](#cite_note-232)
  + "widespread failures in [financial regulation](/wiki/Financial_regulation "Financial regulation") and supervision", including the Federal Reserve's failure to stem the tide of [toxic assets](/wiki/Toxic_asset "Toxic asset").
  + "dramatic failures of [corporate governance](/wiki/Corporate_governance "Corporate governance") and [risk management](/wiki/Risk_management "Risk management") at many systemically important [financial institutions](/wiki/Financial_institutions "Financial institutions")" including too many financial firms acting recklessly and taking on too much risk.
  + "a combination of excessive borrowing, risky investments, and lack of transparency" by financial institutions and by households that put the financial system on a collision course with crisis.
  + ill preparation and inconsistent action by government and key policy makers lacking a full understanding of the financial system they oversaw that "added to the uncertainty and panic".
  + a "systemic breakdown in accountability and ethics" at all levels.
  + "collapsing mortgage-lending standards and the mortgage securitization pipeline".
  + deregulation of '[over-the-counter](/wiki/Over-the-counter_(finance) "Over-the-counter (finance)")' [derivatives](/wiki/Derivative_(finance) "Derivative (finance)"), especially [credit default swaps](/wiki/Credit_default_swap "Credit default swap").
  + "the failures of credit rating agencies" to correctly price risk.
* "[Wall Street and the Financial Crisis: Anatomy of a Financial Collapse](/wiki/Wall_Street_and_the_Financial_Crisis:_Anatomy_of_a_Financial_Collapse "Wall Street and the Financial Crisis: Anatomy of a Financial Collapse")" (known as the Levin–Coburn Report) by the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") concluded that the crisis was the result of "high risk, complex financial products; undisclosed conflicts of interest; the failure of regulators, the credit rating agencies, and the market itself to rein in the excesses of Wall Street".[[233]](#cite_note-233)
* The high delinquency and default rates by homeowners, particularly those with subprime credit, led to a rapid devaluation of mortgage-backed securities including bundled loan portfolios, derivatives and credit default swaps. As the value of these assets plummeted, buyers for these securities evaporated and banks who were heavily invested in these assets began to experience a liquidity crisis.
* [Securitization](/wiki/Securitization "Securitization"), a process in which many mortgages were bundled together and formed into new financial instruments called [mortgage-backed securities](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_securities "Mortgage-backed securities"), allowed for shifting of risk and lax underwriting standards.
