In 2005, at a celebration honoring [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan"), who was about to retire as chairman of the [US Federal Reserve](/wiki/US_Federal_Reserve "US Federal Reserve"), Rajan delivered a controversial paper that was critical of the financial sector.[[413]](#cite_note-413) In that paper, Rajan "argued that disaster might loom".[[414]](#cite_note-414) Rajan argued that financial sector managers were encouraged to "take risks that generate severe adverse consequences with small probability but, in return, offer generous compensation the rest of the time. These risks are known as tail risks. But perhaps the most important concern is whether banks will be able to provide liquidity to financial markets so that if the tail risk does materialize, financial positions can be unwound and losses allocated so that the consequences to the real economy are minimized."

Stock trader and financial risk engineer [Nassim Nicholas Taleb](/wiki/Nassim_Nicholas_Taleb "Nassim Nicholas Taleb"), author of the 2007 book *[The Black Swan](/wiki/The_Black_Swan:_The_Impact_of_the_Highly_Improbable "The Black Swan: The Impact of the Highly Improbable")*, spent years warning against the breakdown of the banking system in particular and the economy in general owing to their use of and reliance on bad risk models and reliance on forecasting, and framed the problem as part of "robustness and fragility".[[415]](#cite_note-415)[[416]](#cite_note-416) He also took action against the establishment view by making a big financial bet on banking stocks and making a fortune from the crisis ("They didn't listen, so I took their money").[[417]](#cite_note-417) According to [David Brooks](/wiki/David_Brooks_(commentator) "David Brooks (commentator)") from *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*, "Taleb not only has an explanation for what's happening, he saw it coming."[[418]](#cite_note-418)

Popular articles published in the mass media have led the general public to believe that the majority of economists have failed in their obligation to predict the financial crisis. For example, an article in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* noted that economist [Nouriel Roubini](/wiki/Nouriel_Roubini "Nouriel Roubini") warned of such crisis as early as September 2006, and stated that the profession of economics is bad at predicting recessions.[[419]](#cite_note-419) According to *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")*, Roubini was ridiculed for predicting a collapse of the housing market and worldwide recession, while *The New York Times* labelled him "Dr. Doom".[[420]](#cite_note-420)

In a 2012 article in the journal *[Japan and the World Economy](/wiki/Japan_and_the_World_Economy "Japan and the World Economy")*, Andrew K. Rose and Mark M. Spiegel used a Multiple Indicator Multiple Cause (MIMIC) model on a cross-section of 107 countries to evaluate potential causes of the 2008 crisis. The authors examined various economic indicators, ignoring [contagion effects](/wiki/Financial_contagion "Financial contagion") across countries. The authors concluded: "We include over sixty potential causes of the crisis, covering such categories as: financial system policies and conditions; asset price appreciation in real estate and equity markets; international imbalances and foreign reserve adequacy; macroeconomic policies; and institutional and geographic features. Despite the fact that we use a wide number of possible causes in a flexible statistical framework, we are unable to link most of the commonly cited causes of the crisis to its incidence across countries. This negative finding in the cross-section makes us skeptical of the accuracy of 'early warning' systems of potential crises, which must also predict their timing."[[421]](#cite_note-421)

## IndyMac

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: IndyMac")]

The first visible institution to run into trouble in the United States was the Southern California–based [IndyMac](/wiki/IndyMac "IndyMac"), a spin-off of [Countrywide Financial](/wiki/Countrywide_Financial "Countrywide Financial"). Before its failure, IndyMac Bank was the largest [savings and loan association](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association") in the Los Angeles market and the seventh-largest [mortgage loan](/wiki/Mortgage_loan "Mortgage loan") originator in the United States.[[422]](#cite_note-422) The failure of IndyMac Bank on July 11, 2008, was (until the crisis precipitated even larger failures) the fourth-largest [bank failure](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") in United States history,[[423]](#cite_note-423) and the second-largest failure of a regulated [thrift](/wiki/Savings_and_loan_association "Savings and loan association").[[424]](#cite_note-shutsdown-424) IndyMac Bank's parent corporation was IndyMac Bancorp until the FDIC seized IndyMac Bank.[[425]](#cite_note-Liquidate-425) IndyMac Bancorp filed for [Chapter 7 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_7_bankruptcy "Chapter 7 bankruptcy") in July 2008.[[425]](#cite_note-Liquidate-425)

IndyMac Bank was founded as Countrywide Mortgage Investment in 1985 by [David S. Loeb](/wiki/David_S._Loeb "David S. Loeb") and [Angelo Mozilo](/wiki/Angelo_Mozilo "Angelo Mozilo")[[426]](#cite_note-426)[[427]](#cite_note-427) as a means of [collateralizing](/wiki/Cross-collateralization "Cross-collateralization") [Countrywide Financial](/wiki/Countrywide_Financial "Countrywide Financial") loans too big to be sold to Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae. In 1997, Countrywide spun off IndyMac as an independent company run by Mike Perry, who remained its CEO until the downfall of the bank in July 2008.[[428]](#cite_note-Paletta-428)

The primary causes of its failure were largely associated with its business strategy of originating and securitizing [Alt-A](/wiki/Alt-A "Alt-A") loans on a large scale. This strategy resulted in rapid growth and a high concentration of risky assets. From its inception as a savings association in 2000, IndyMac grew to the seventh largest savings and loan and ninth largest originator of mortgage loans in the United States. During 2006, IndyMac originated over $90 billion (~$134 billion in 2024) of mortgages.

IndyMac's aggressive growth strategy, use of Alt-A and other nontraditional loan products, insufficient underwriting, credit concentrations in residential real estate in the [California](/wiki/California "California") and [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") markets—states, alongside Nevada and Arizona, where the [housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble") was most pronounced—and heavy reliance on costly funds borrowed from a [Federal Home Loan Bank](/wiki/Federal_Home_Loan_Bank "Federal Home Loan Bank") (FHLB) and from brokered deposits, led to its demise when the mortgage market declined in 2007.

IndyMac often made loans without verification of the borrower's income or assets, and to borrowers with poor credit histories. Appraisals obtained by IndyMac on underlying collateral were often questionable as well. As an Alt-A lender, IndyMac's business model was to offer loan products to fit the borrower's needs, using an extensive array of risky option-[adjustable-rate mortgages](/wiki/Adjustable-rate_mortgage "Adjustable-rate mortgage") (option ARMs), subprime loans, 80/20 loans, and other nontraditional products. Ultimately, loans were made to many borrowers who simply could not afford to make their payments. The thrift remained profitable only as long as it was able to sell those loans in the [secondary mortgage market](/wiki/Secondary_mortgage_market "Secondary mortgage market"). IndyMac resisted efforts to regulate its involvement in those loans or tighten their issuing criteria: see the comment by Ruthann Melbourne, Chief Risk Officer, to the regulating agencies.[[429]](#cite_note-lossreview-429)[[430]](#cite_note-430)[[431]](#cite_note-431)

On May 12, 2008, in the "Capital" section of its last 10-Q, [IndyMac](/wiki/IndyMac "IndyMac") revealed that it may not be well capitalized in the future.[[432]](#cite_note-indymac10Q-432)

IndyMac reported that during April 2008, [Moody's](/wiki/Moody%27s "Moody's") and [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's") downgraded the ratings on a significant number of [Mortgage-backed security](/wiki/Mortgage-backed_security "Mortgage-backed security") (MBS) bonds—including $160 million (~$228 million in 2024) issued by IndyMac that the bank retained in its MBS portfolio. IndyMac concluded that these downgrades would have harmed its risk-based capital ratio as of June 30, 2008. Had these lowered ratings been in effect on March 31, 2008, IndyMac concluded that the bank's capital ratio would have been 9.27% total risk-based. IndyMac warned that if its regulators found its capital position to have fallen below "well capitalized" (minimum 10% risk-based capital ratio) to "adequately capitalized" (8–10% risk-based capital ratio) the bank might no longer be able to use brokered deposits as a source of funds.

Senator [Charles Schumer](/wiki/Charles_Schumer "Charles Schumer") (D-NY) later pointed out that brokered deposits made up more than 37% of IndyMac's total deposits, and asked the [Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation](/wiki/Federal_Deposit_Insurance_Corporation "Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation") (FDIC) whether it had considered ordering IndyMac to reduce its reliance on these deposits.[[433]](#cite_note-433) With $18.9 billion in total deposits reported on March 31,[[432]](#cite_note-indymac10Q-432) Senator Schumer would have been referring to a little over $7 billion in brokered deposits. While the breakout of maturities of these deposits is not known exactly, a simple averaging would have put the threat of brokered deposits loss to IndyMac at $500 million a month, had the regulator disallowed IndyMac from acquiring new brokered deposits on June 30.

IndyMac was taking new measures to preserve capital, such as deferring interest payments on some preferred securities. Dividends on common shares had already been suspended for the first quarter of 2008, after being cut in half the previous quarter. The company still had not secured a significant capital infusion nor found a ready buyer.[[434]](#cite_note-434)

IndyMac reported that the bank's risk-based capital was only $47 million above the minimum required for this 10% mark. But it did not reveal some of that $47 million (~$67 million in 2024) capital it claimed it had, as of March 31, 2008, was fabricated.[[435]](#cite_note-435)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Wikinews-logo.svg/40px-Wikinews-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikinews-logo.svg)

Wikinews has related news:

* [IndyMac Bank placed into conservatorship by US Government](https://en.wikinews.org/wiki/Category:July_15,_2008#IndyMac_Bank_placed_into_conservatorship_by_US_Government "wikinews:Category:July 15, 2008")

When home prices declined in the latter half of 2007 and the secondary mortgage market collapsed, IndyMac was forced to hold $10.7 billion (~$15.5 billion in 2024) of loans it could not sell in the secondary market. Its reduced liquidity was further exacerbated in late June 2008 when account holders withdrew $1.55 billion (~$2.21 billion in 2024) or about 7.5% of IndyMac's deposits.[[432]](#cite_note-indymac10Q-432) This [bank run](/wiki/Bank_run "Bank run") on the thrift followed the public release of a letter from Senator Charles Schumer to the FDIC and OTS. The letter outlined the Senator's concerns with IndyMac. While the run was a contributing factor in the timing of IndyMac's demise, the underlying cause of the failure was the unsafe and unsound way it was operated.[[429]](#cite_note-lossreview-429)

On June 26, 2008, Senator [Charles Schumer](/wiki/Charles_Schumer "Charles Schumer") (D-NY), a member of the [Senate Banking Committee](/wiki/Senate_Banking_Committee "Senate Banking Committee"), chairman of Congress' [Joint Economic Committee](/wiki/Joint_Economic_Committee "Joint Economic Committee") and the third-ranking Democrat in the Senate, released several letters he had sent to regulators, in which he was"concerned that IndyMac's financial deterioration poses significant risks to both taxpayers and borrowers." Some worried depositors began to withdraw money.[[436]](#cite_note-436)[[437]](#cite_note-437)

On July 7, 2008, IndyMac announced on the company blog that it:

* Had failed to raise [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)") since its May 12, 2008, quarterly earnings report;
* Had been notified by bank and thrift regulators that IndyMac Bank was no longer deemed "well-capitalized".

IndyMac announced the closure of both its retail lending and wholesale divisions, halted new loan submissions, and cut 3,800 jobs.[[438]](#cite_note-438)

On July 11, 2008, citing liquidity concerns, the FDIC put IndyMac Bank into [conservatorship](/wiki/Conservatorship "Conservatorship"). A [bridge bank](/wiki/Bridge_bank_(United_States) "Bridge bank (United States)"), IndyMac Federal Bank, FSB, was established to assume control of IndyMac Bank's assets, its secured liabilities, and its insured deposit accounts. The FDIC announced plans to open IndyMac Federal Bank, FSB on July 14, 2008. Until then, depositors would have access to their insured deposits through ATMs, their existing checks, and their existing debit cards. Telephone and Internet account access was restored when the bank reopened.[[125]](#cite_note-indymactaken-125)[[439]](#cite_note-439)[[440]](#cite_note-fn1-440) The FDIC guarantees the funds of all insured accounts up to US$100,000, and declared a special advance dividend to the roughly 10,000 depositors with funds in excess of the insured amount, guaranteeing 50% of any amounts in excess of $100,000.[[424]](#cite_note-shutsdown-424) Yet, even with the pending sale of Indymac to IMB Management Holdings, an estimated 10,000 uninsured depositors of Indymac are still at a loss of over $270 million.[[441]](#cite_note-441)[[442]](#cite_note-442)

With $32 billion in assets, IndyMac Bank was one of the largest bank failures in American history.[[443]](#cite_note-443)

IndyMac Bancorp filed for [Chapter 7 bankruptcy](/wiki/Chapter_7_bankruptcy "Chapter 7 bankruptcy") on July 31, 2008.[[425]](#cite_note-Liquidate-425)

Initially the companies affected were those directly involved in home construction and mortgage lending such as Northern Rock and [Countrywide Financial](/wiki/Countrywide_Financial "Countrywide Financial"), as they could no longer obtain financing through the credit markets. Over 100 mortgage lenders went bankrupt during 2007 and 2008. Concerns that investment bank [Bear Stearns](/wiki/Bear_Stearns "Bear Stearns") would collapse in March 2008 resulted in its fire-sale to [JP Morgan Chase](/wiki/JP_Morgan_Chase "JP Morgan Chase"). The financial institution crisis hit its peak in September and October 2008. Several major institutions either failed, were acquired under duress, or were subject to government takeover. These included [Lehman Brothers](/wiki/Lehman_Brothers "Lehman Brothers"), [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch"), Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac, [Washington Mutual](/wiki/Washington_Mutual "Washington Mutual"), [Wachovia](/wiki/Wachovia "Wachovia"), [Citigroup](/wiki/Citigroup "Citigroup"), and [AIG](/wiki/AIG "AIG").[[32]](#cite_note-greatcrash-32) On October 6, 2008, three weeks after Lehman Brothers filed the largest bankruptcy in U.S. history, Lehman's former CEO [Richard S. Fuld Jr.](/wiki/Richard_S._Fuld_Jr. "Richard S. Fuld Jr.") found himself before Representative [Henry A. Waxman](/wiki/Henry_A._Waxman "Henry A. Waxman"), the California Democrat who chaired the [House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform](/wiki/House_Committee_on_Oversight_and_Government_Reform "House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform"). Fuld said he was a victim of the collapse, blaming a "crisis of confidence" in the markets for dooming his firm.[[444]](#cite_note-444)

## Notable books and movies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2008_financial_crisis&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Notable books and movies")]

* In 2006, [Peter Schiff](/wiki/Peter_Schiff "Peter Schiff") authored a book titled *[Crash Proof: How to Profit From the Coming Economic Collapse](/wiki/Crash_Proof:_How_to_Profit_From_the_Coming_Economic_Collapse "Crash Proof: How to Profit From the Coming Economic Collapse")*, which was published in February 2007 by [Wiley](/wiki/Wiley_(publisher) "Wiley (publisher)"). The book describes various features of the economy and housing market that led to the [United States housing bubble](/wiki/United_States_housing_bubble "United States housing bubble"), and warns of the impending decline. After many of the predictions came to pass, a second edition titled *Crash Proof 2.0* was published in 2009, which included a "2009 update" addendum at the end of each chapter. It was featured on [The New York Times Best Seller list](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list").
* *[Meltdown: A Free-Market Look at Why the Stock Market Collapsed, the Economy Tanked, and the Government Bailout Will Make Things Worse](/wiki/Meltdown_(Woods_book) "Meltdown (Woods book)")*, by [Tom Woods](/wiki/Tom_Woods "Tom Woods"), was published in February 2009 by [Regnery Publishing](/wiki/Regnery_Publishing "Regnery Publishing"). It was featured on [The New York Times Best Seller list](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Best_Seller_list "The New York Times Best Seller list") for 10 weeks.
* The American documentary program *[Frontline](/wiki/Frontline_(American_TV_program) "Frontline (American TV program)")* produced several episodes investigating various aspects of the crisis:
  + "[Inside the Meltdown](https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/documentary/meltdown/?)" (Season 2009: Episode 8)
  + "[Ten Trillion and Counting](https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/documentary/tentrillion/?)" (Season 2009: Episode 9)
  + "[Breaking the Bank](https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/documentary/breakingthebank/?)" (Season 2009: Episode 15)
  + "[The Warning](https://www.pbs.org/wgbh/frontline/documentary/warning/?)" (Season 2009: Episode 2)
* A 2010 documentary film, *Overdose: A Film about the Next Financial Crisis*, describes how the financial crisis came about and how the solutions that have been applied by many governments are setting the stage for the next crisis. The film is based on the book *Financial Fiasco* by [Johan Norberg](/wiki/Johan_Norberg "Johan Norberg") and features [Alan Greenspan](/wiki/Alan_Greenspan "Alan Greenspan"), with funding from the [libertarian](/wiki/Libertarian "Libertarian") think tank [Cato Institute](/wiki/Cato_Institute "Cato Institute").[[445]](#cite_note-445)
* In October 2010, a documentary film about the crisis, *[Inside Job](/wiki/Inside_Job_(2010_film) "Inside Job (2010 film)")* directed by [Charles Ferguson](/wiki/Charles_Ferguson_(filmmaker) "Charles Ferguson (filmmaker)"), was released by [Sony Pictures Classics](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Classics "Sony Pictures Classics"). In 2011, it won the [Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Documentary_Feature "Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature") at the [83rd Academy Awards](/wiki/83rd_Academy_Awards "83rd Academy Awards").[[446]](#cite_note-446)
* Set on the night before the crisis broke, the 2011 film *[Margin Call](/wiki/Margin_Call "Margin Call")* is a movie that follows traders through a sleepless 24 hours as they try to contain the damage after an analyst discovers information that is likely to ruin their firm, and possibly the whole economy.[[447]](#cite_note-447)
* The 2011 film *[Too Big to Fail](/wiki/Too_Big_to_Fail_(film) "Too Big to Fail (film)")* is based on [Andrew Ross Sorkin](/wiki/Andrew_Ross_Sorkin "Andrew Ross Sorkin")'s 2009 non-fiction book *[Too Big to Fail: The Inside Story of How Wall Street and Washington Fought to Save the Financial System – and Themselves](/wiki/Too_Big_to_Fail_(book) "Too Big to Fail (book)")*.
* [Michael Lewis](/wiki/Michael_Lewis "Michael Lewis") authored a best-selling non-fiction book about the crisis, entitled *[The Big Short](/wiki/The_Big_Short "The Big Short")*. In 2015, it was adapted into a [film of the same name](/wiki/The_Big_Short_(film) "The Big Short (film)"), which won the [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay"), [BAFTA Award](/wiki/BAFTA_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "BAFTA Award for Best Adapted Screenplay"), [Critics' Choice Movie Award](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Critics' Choice Movie Award for Best Adapted Screenplay"), and [Writers Guild of America Award](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America_Award_for_Best_Adapted_Screenplay "Writers Guild of America Award for Best Adapted Screenplay"), all for Best Adapted Screenplay. One point raised is to what extent those outside of the markets themselves (i.e., not working for a mainstream investment bank) could forecast the events and be generally less myopic.
* Simon Reid-Henry's 2019 book *[Empire of Democracy](/wiki/Empire_of_Democracy "Empire of Democracy")* describes how liberal norms[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] in the West were replaced by populism as a consequence of the 2007–08 financial crisis, as well as [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") policies[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] that had emerged in previous decades which hollowed out government and changed voter expectations.
* [Virág Blazsek](/wiki/Vir%C3%A1g_Blazsek "Virág Blazsek")'s book, *Banking Bailout Law: A Comparative Study of the United States, United Kingdom and the European Union* (Routledge, 2020) examines the 2008 Global Financial Crisis-related bank bailout and resolution techniques through case studies from the United States, the EU, the United Kingdom, Spain and Hungary. The book identifies the advantages and disadvantages of different legal and regulatory approaches and proposes a regulatory framework intended to better address future financial crises.[[448]](#cite_note-448)
